text: In Plum Village, the community Thich Nhat Hanh founded in France, a bell rings randomly throughout the day. Every activity stops; everyone breathes three conscious breaths; then life resumes. The bell does not announce importance — it interrupts the assumption that the present moment can wait. Hanh borrowed this practice from Vietnamese village life, where temple bells once structured communal attention. Transposed to a modern context, the bell becomes resistance to what he called "running" — the habitual forward-leaning of the mind that evacuates the present. The practice is architectural: not relying on individual willpower but building interruption into the environment itself.
question: According to the passage, why does the bell at Plum Village ring randomly rather than at scheduled times?

options.text: To mirror the unpredictability of traditional Vietnamese village rhythms
To interrupt the assumption that the present moment can wait and return attention to now
To signal transitions between different periods of monastic practice
To train practitioners to maintain awareness despite external disruption
